name: Commander's Palace
description: The grand dame of New Orleans dining — a Garden District institution since 1893. Legendary Saturday jazz brunch, outstanding turtle soup, and a wine list that could fill a library. Dress code enforced; reservations essential.

name: Dooky Chase's
description: Leah Chase's legendary Treme restaurant — where civil rights leaders ate, presidents dined, and Gumbo z'Herbes has been served every Holy Thursday for decades. A pillar of Black New Orleans culture and one of the city's most important tables.

name: Galatoire's
description: Unchanged since 1905, this French Quarter institution is New Orleans dining at its most theatrical. Regulars occupy the same tables for decades. Order the shrimp remoulade, trout meunière, and soufflé potatoes. Friday lunch is a city tradition.

name: Café Du Monde
description: Open since 1862, this French Market institution serves exactly two things: beignets buried under powdered sugar and café au lait made with chicory. Go at 2am for the full experience. Outdoor seating overlooks Jackson Square.

name: Brennan's
description: The birthplace of Bananas Foster and the New Orleans power brunch. The iconic pink building on Royal Street has been serving extravagant morning meals since 1946. Order the turtle soup, eggs Sardou, and — obviously — Bananas Foster tableside.

name: Willie Mae's Scotch House
description: James Beard America's Classic. Willie Mae Seaton's fried chicken — light batter, impossibly juicy — is the benchmark by which all others are judged. Treme institution with cash-only policy and lines that move faster than they look.
address: 2401 St Ann St, New Orleans, LA 70119

name: Parkway Bakery & Tavern
description: The definitive New Orleans po-boy shop, open since 1911. The roast beef debris po-boy — gravy-soaked shredded meat on Leidenheimer bread — is the standard. Order it 'dressed' and don't think twice.

name: Dong Phuong
description: New Orleans East's Vietnamese bakery and restaurant, beloved for King Cake during Mardi Gras and bánh mì year-round. The baguettes are exceptional — a direct result of Louisiana's French colonial bread traditions meeting Vietnamese technique.
address: 14207 Chef Menteur Hwy, New Orleans, LA 70129

name: August
description: John Besh's flagship in a converted 19th-century tobacco warehouse near the CBD. Modern Louisiana fine dining executed with precision — Gulf fish, local produce, and one of the best cheese programs in the South.

name: Coquette
description: Neighborhood bistro on Magazine Street that consistently outperforms its low-key setting. Seasonal menu with strong Louisiana sourcing, excellent natural wine list, and some of the most technically precise cooking in the city.

name: Compère Lapin
description: Nina Compton's James Beard Award-winning restaurant in the Old No. 77 Hotel. Caribbean-meets-Louisiana cuisine at its most inventive — curried goat with sweet potato gnocchi is a signature. One of the best brunches in the city.

name: Mahony's Po-Boys
description: Uptown's go-to po-boy shop with creative spins beyond the classics — the surf and turf (roast beef and fried shrimp) is essential. Uses Leidenheimer bread, makes their own remoulade, and takes the craft seriously.

name: Cure
description: The bar that launched the New Orleans cocktail renaissance when it opened in 2009. Serious, ingredient-driven drinks in a beautifully restored firehouse on Freret Street. Knowledgeable bartenders, excellent amaro selection, and zero pretension.

name: French 75 Bar at Arnaud's
description: One of the great bar rooms in America — pressed tin ceiling, vintage portraits, and white-jacketed bartenders who take the French 75 as seriously as a religion. Order the namesake cocktail and nothing else.

name: Carousel Bar at Hotel Monteleone
description: The bar that slowly rotates. Beloved New Orleans landmark in the lobby of the Hotel Monteleone — a 25-seat circular bar that completes one revolution every 15 minutes. Order a Vieux Carré (invented here) and let the French Quarter drift past.

name: Bacchanal Wine
description: Bywater wine shop and backyard bar with live jazz every night. Buy a bottle off the shelf, pay a small corkage, and settle in for hours. The food — cheese plates, charcuterie, small plates — is better than it needs to be. One of the city's best hangs.

name: The Sazerac Bar
description: Home of the Sazerac — New Orleans' official cocktail and arguably America's first. The gorgeous art deco bar inside the Roosevelt Hotel has Paul Ninas murals and bartenders who respect the rye-absinthe-Peychaud's ratio like scripture.

name: French Truck Coffee
description: New Orleans' best specialty roaster, with beans sourced from small farms and roasted in-house. The cold brew rivals anything in the country. Magazine Street flagship is the place to work for an afternoon — excellent pour-overs, fast wifi.

name: Louisiana Music Factory
description: The anchor of the Frenchmen Street music district, selling new and used vinyl with deep stock in New Orleans jazz, zydeco, brass band, R&B, and second-line. Staff actually know music. Hosts in-store performances during Jazz Fest.

name: Peaches Records
description: Independent record store in the Uptown neighborhood, stocked deep in New Orleans funk, soul, jazz, and local releases you won't find elsewhere. A neighborhood staple for decades with a strong community ethos.

name: Middendorf's
description: Manchac roadhouse open since 1934, perched over the swamp on Lake Maurepas. The legendary thin-fried catfish — a half-inch fillet fried until lacework-crispy — is the reason for the 45-minute drive from the city. Order the large, get extra tartar sauce.

name: Toup's Meatery
description: Isaac Toups' nose-to-tail Cajun butcher shop and restaurant in Lafayette — the capital of Cajun country, about 90 minutes west. Boudin, cracklins, and cochon de lait made with encyclopedic knowledge of Louisiana hog traditions. A mandatory stop heading into Acadiana.

name: Oxbow Rum Distillery
description: Small-batch rum distillery in Madisonville on the Tchefuncte River, about 50 minutes from New Orleans via the Causeway. Tours and tastings in a beautifully restored building. The aged expressions are exceptional — pick up a bottle of the Pontchartrain.

name: Abita Brewery
description: Louisiana's landmark craft brewery in Abita Springs, about 45 minutes north across the Lake Pontchartrain Causeway. Tap room pours the full lineup — including seasonal and small-batch releases unavailable elsewhere. The tour is casual, the Purple Haze is cold.

name: Café des Amis
description: Breaux Bridge institution at the heart of the Cajun Heartland, about 45 minutes past Lafayette on the Bayou Teche. Famous for the Saturday Zydeco Breakfast — live zydeco music starting at 8am while you eat crawfish étouffée and boudin. Nothing else like it.
address: 140 E Bridge St, Breaux Bridge, LA 70517

name: Frenchmen Street
description: The beating heart of New Orleans live music — a two-block stretch in the Marigny where jazz, brass band, funk, and blues spill out of clubs every night. The Spotted Cat, dba, and Snug Harbor anchor the strip. Skip Bourbon Street; come here.

name: Jackson Square
description: The anchor of the French Quarter — a National Historic Landmark framed by the St. Louis Cathedral, Pontalba Buildings, and the Mississippi River levee. Artists, tarot readers, and street musicians fill the square daily. The view from the river side at golden hour is the city's postcard.

name: St. Louis Cathedral
description: The oldest continuously active Roman Catholic cathedral in the United States, dating to 1718. The triple steeple silhouette over Jackson Square is one of America's most iconic views. Free to visit; the interior is unexpectedly serene amid the French Quarter chaos.
address: 615 Pere Antoine Alley, New Orleans, LA 70116

name: New Orleans Museum of Art
description: The city's premier fine art museum in City Park, with a permanent collection spanning 5,000 years and 40,000 objects. The adjacent Sydney and Walda Besthoff Sculpture Garden is free and one of the best outdoor art spaces in the South. Don't miss the glass collection.
address: 1 Collins Diboll Cir, New Orleans, LA 70124

name: National WWII Museum
description: The definitive American WWII museum — one of the most visited museums in the country and ranked among the world's best. The Road to Berlin and Road to Tokyo pavilions are immersive and emotionally powerful. Plan at least four hours; the 4D film with Tom Hanks is worth it.

name: The Ogden Museum of Southern Art
description: The world's largest collection of Southern art, housed across two connected buildings on Lee Circle. Rotating and permanent exhibitions cover painting, photography, folk art, and sculpture. Thursday night Ogden After Hours series pairs live music with the galleries.

name: City Park
description: 1,300 acres of old-growth live oaks, lagoons, and gardens — one of the oldest and largest urban parks in the country. Home to NOMA, the botanical garden, a carousel built in 1906, pedalboats, and miles of trails. The ancient oaks draped in Spanish moss are otherworldly.

name: New Orleans Botanical Garden
description: Ten acres of formal gardens inside City Park featuring one of the finest collections of WPA-era garden architecture in the country. Tropical conservatory, rose garden, and extraordinary live oak allée. Quiet and beautiful on a weekday morning.

name: Preservation Hall
description: The most important room in American music. Intimate, un-air-conditioned, standing-room hall in the French Quarter where traditional New Orleans jazz has been performed nightly since 1961. No drinks, no food, no frills — just the music. The late set after 10pm is the one to catch.
address: 726 St Peter St, New Orleans, LA 70116

name: Snug Harbor Jazz Bistro
description: The city's premier modern jazz club on Frenchmen Street, with serious bookings — Ellis Marsalis held a Friday night residency here for decades. Two shows nightly; the listening room format means the music is the focus. Reserve ahead for weekend sets.

name: Magazine Street
description: Six miles of independently owned boutiques, galleries, cafes, restaurants, and antique shops stretching from the CBD through the Garden District and Uptown. The antique corridor between Louisiana and Napoleon avenues is particularly strong. Better shopping than any mall in Louisiana.

name: Lafayette Cemetery No. 1
description: New Orleans' famous above-ground cemetery in the Garden District, dating to 1833. Shaded by ancient oaks, the brick-and-plaster family tombs form a city-within-a-city. Free to enter; the Save Our Cemeteries tours are worth it for the history.

name: St. Louis Cemetery No. 1
description: The oldest extant cemetery in New Orleans, dating to 1789, and the most famous — Marie Laveau is buried here. Access now requires a guided tour (required since 2015 to prevent vandalism). Haunting, historic, and unlike any burial ground in America.
address: 615 St Louis St, New Orleans, LA 70112

name: The Spotted Cat Music Club
description: No cover, no pretension, no bad nights. The Spotted Cat is the most beloved live music room on Frenchmen Street — a small, warm bar where swing, jazz, and brass bands play from early afternoon into the early morning. Go multiple nights.

name: Bayou St. John
description: A narrow urban bayou connecting Mid-City to Lake Pontchartrain, beloved for Sunday afternoon hangs — locals bring floaties, cold drinks, and dogs. The bayou-side path is one of the city's best walks. The drawbridge at Magnolia is a classic New Orleans scene.
address: Bayou St John, New Orleans, LA 70119

name: Audubon Zoo
description: One of the top-rated zoos in America, situated in Audubon Park along the Uptown riverfront. Strong on Louisiana wildlife — white alligators, swamp exhibits, and a Louisiana Swamp display unique to any zoo in the country. The streetcar ride up St. Charles is part of the experience.

name: The Fly (Audubon Park Riverside)
description: The grassy river batture at the end of Audubon Park where locals gather to watch massive cargo ships drift by impossibly close. Bring a blanket, a bottle of wine, and nowhere to be. One of the great free hangouts in the city.

name: New Orleans Jazz Museum
description: Located in the historic Old U.S. Mint on the edge of the French Quarter, this museum documents the birthplace of jazz with instruments, recordings, and ephemera spanning 125 years. Free live performances Thursday–Saturday. The Louis Armstrong collection is the centerpiece.

name: St. Charles Avenue Streetcar
description: The oldest continuously operating street railway system in the world (since 1835) and a National Historic Landmark. Ride it from the CBD all the way to the Carrollton turnaround through the Garden District and Uptown, passing the grandest mansions in New Orleans. $1.25 each way.
address: St Charles Ave & Canal St, New Orleans, LA 70130

name: Steamboat Natchez
description: The last authentic steamboat on the Mississippi River, departing from the French Quarter for two-hour harbor cruises with live jazz. The steam calliope plays before departure — you'll hear it blocks away. The evening dinner cruise is the most atmospheric way to see the city from the water.

name: Backstreet Cultural Museum
description: The definitive archive of Mardi Gras Indian culture, second-line tradition, and Black Carnival in New Orleans. Sequined suits, feathered crowns, and decades of photographs fill a small Treme shotgun house. Curated by the late Sylvester Francis — irreplaceable and humbling.
address: 1116 St Claude Ave, New Orleans, LA 70116

name: Louis Armstrong Park
description: A 32-acre park on the edge of Treme anchored by a monumental bronze of Satchmo. Congo Square — where enslaved people gathered to drum and dance on Sundays, the birthplace of American music — sits inside the gates. Jazz Fest overflow stages set up here during festival week.
address: 835 N Rampart St, New Orleans, LA 70116

name: Mardi Gras World
description: The warehouse where Blaine Kern's artists build the enormous float sculptures for Mardi Gras parades. Year-round tours walk you through floats in progress, prop storage, and the full history of Carnival. Surreal scale — the kings and queens are five stories tall.
address: 1380 Port of New Orleans Pl, New Orleans, LA 70130

name: Aquarium of the Americas
description: Excellent riverfront aquarium with a standout Gulf of Mexico exhibit and white alligator display. The Amazon rainforest tunnel and Caribbean reef tank are the highlights. Well worth a morning, especially with kids — the location on the river is ideal.

name: The Contemporary Arts Center
description: The anchor of the Warehouse Arts District, occupying a beautifully converted 1905 warehouse. Four floors of rotating exhibitions spanning visual art, performance, and film. The programming is genuinely adventurous — check what's up before visiting.

name: The Sazerac House
description: Showroom and museum for the Sazerac Company — home of the Sazerac rye, Buffalo Trace bourbon, and Peychaud's bitters. Free self-guided tours through four floors of cocktail history, distilling demos, and an exceptionally well-designed whiskey education center. Free to enter.

name: The French Market
description: America's oldest public market, in continuous operation since 1791 along the Mississippi River. The covered flea market section is best for local crafts, vintage, and oddities — skip the tourist tchotchke stalls and dig deeper. Farmers market on weekends is the best food shopping in the Quarter.
address: 1235 N Peters St, New Orleans, LA 70116

name: Chalmette Battlefield (Jean Lafitte NHP)
description: Site of Andrew Jackson's improbable 1815 victory in the Battle of New Orleans — the final major battle of the War of 1812, fought two weeks after the peace treaty was signed. Free NPS site about 20 minutes east of downtown. The long grassy field and river views are unexpectedly moving.
address: 8606 W St Bernard Hwy, Chalmette, LA 70043

name: Honey Island Swamp Tour
description: One of the least-altered river swamps in the country, about 45 minutes northeast of New Orleans near Slidell. Guided flat-bottom boat tours through cypress-tupelo swamp teeming with alligators, herons, and river otters. Dr. Paul Wagner's original tour is the gold standard.

name: Barataria Preserve (Jean Lafitte NHP)
description: 23,000 acres of undeveloped Louisiana wetlands — swamp, marsh, and bottomland forest — about 30 minutes south of New Orleans. Eight miles of boardwalk trails through old-growth cypress swamp with alligators sunning at arm's length. Go early morning for birds and mist.

name: Oak Alley Plantation
description: The most photogenic plantation in Louisiana — a quarter-mile canopy of 300-year-old live oaks leads to the 1839 Greek Revival mansion on the River Road, about 55 minutes west of New Orleans. The Slavery at Oak Alley exhibit is unflinching and important. Tours required.

name: Laura Plantation
description: Creole plantation on the River Road, about 50 minutes from New Orleans, with the most historically rigorous tour in Louisiana. Unlike Greek Revival mansions, the Creole architecture tells the story of French and African cultural fusion. The enslaved quarters and Br'er Rabbit oral tradition documentation are exceptional.

name: Fontainebleau State Park
description: 2,800-acre state park on the north shore of Lake Pontchartrain in Mandeville, about 45 minutes from New Orleans. A long swimming beach, ruins of an 1829 sugar mill, live oak forest, and excellent birding. The lakefront sunsets are spectacular. Camping available.

name: Global Wildlife Center
description: 900-acre free-range wildlife preserve in Folsom, about an hour north of New Orleans. Safari wagons roll through open pastures where giraffes, zebras, bison, and dozens of other species roam freely and eat from your hand. The giraffe encounters are genuinely extraordinary.

name: Poverty Point World Heritage Site
description: A UNESCO World Heritage Site and one of the most significant archaeological sites in North America — a massive earthwork complex built by a sophisticated hunter-gatherer society around 1700 BCE. About 4.5 hours north, but unmatched in historical weight. The mounds are best seen from the air via drone or observation tower.

name: Fort Pike State Historic Site
description: Early 19th-century masonry fort on the Chef Menteur Pass, about 30 minutes east of New Orleans. Built to defend the city after the War of 1812, later occupied by Confederate and Union forces. Compact, well-preserved, and almost always uncrowded — a genuinely overlooked gem.
address: 27100 Chef Menteur Hwy, New Orleans, LA 70129

name: Turkey & The Wolf
description: Mason Hereford's absurdist sandwich shop that Bon Appétit once called the best new restaurant in America. The collard green melt is a religious experience. Cash-only vibes, long lines, and worth every minute. Bib Gourmand.

name: Cochon
description: Donald Link's love letter to Cajun cooking — whole-hog butchery, wood-fired meats, and Gulf seafood rooted in deep Louisiana tradition. The wood-fired oysters, boudin, and fried rabbit with jalapeño honey are essential. One of the most important restaurants in the South. Bib Gourmand.

name: Cochon Butcher
description: Donald Link's wine bar and charcuterie shop next door to Cochon — exceptional house-cured meats, muffulettas, and meat-heavy sandwiches. One of the best quick lunches in the city. The muffuletta rivals Central Grocery. Bib Gourmand.

name: Domilise's Po-Boy & Bar
description: A cramped, cash-only Uptown shack open since 1918 serving shrimp and roast beef po-boys that inspire devotion. The shrimp po-boy — dressed, on Leidenheimer — is the city benchmark. The bar feels like someone's living room because it basically is. Bib Gourmand.

name: Saba
description: Alon Shaya's Israeli restaurant on Magazine Street — the most personal and assured cooking of his career. Pita baked to order in a wood-burning oven, exceptional hummus, and a menu that draws on Jewish-Israeli and New Orleans Creole traditions simultaneously. Bib Gourmand.

name: Mister Mao
description: Sophina Uong's restless, globe-spanning menu — Thai curry, Sichuan-spiced duck, West African stew — united by Louisiana produce and an insistent point of view. One of the most exciting restaurants in New Orleans right now. The fried chicken sandwich alone justifies the trip. Bib Gourmand.

name: Acamaya
description: Ana Castro's Mexican restaurant in the Marigny — masa-forward cooking rooted in Oaxacan and Yucatecan tradition, executed with New Orleans ingredients. The tlayudas, mole, and agave cocktail program are exceptional. One of the most talked-about openings in recent years. Bib Gourmand.
address: 718 N Rampart St, New Orleans, LA 70116

name: Lufu NOLA
description: Elevated regional Indian cuisine in the CBD — the first Indian restaurant to earn a Michelin Bib Gourmand in New Orleans. Chef Sachin Darade cooks with Louisiana produce and ingredients alongside classical South Asian technique. The cocktail program draws on Indian flavors and is outstanding. Bib Gourmand.
address: 301 St Charles Ave, New Orleans, LA 70130

name: Hungry Eyes
description: Mason Hereford's second restaurant on Magazine Street — Turkish-inflected comfort food in an unabashedly 1980s-themed room. The kebabs, dips, and flatbreads are excellent, and the whole thing moves with the offbeat energy that made Turkey & The Wolf famous. Bib Gourmand.

name: Emeril's
description: Emeril Lagasse's flagship — the only two-Michelin-star restaurant in the American South. Opened in 1990 in the Warehouse District, it remains the most significant restaurant in New Orleans' modern culinary history. The tasting menu showcases Creole technique at its most refined. Two Michelin Stars.

name: Saint-Germain
description: An intimate 12-seat chef's counter in the Marigny offering a single nightly tasting menu — one of the most personal and technically precise dining experiences in New Orleans. Reservations open weeks in advance and go immediately. One Michelin Star.
address: 3054 St Claude Ave, New Orleans, LA 70117

name: Zasu
description: A Mid-City restaurant blending Eastern European culinary tradition with Gulf Coast ingredients in ways that feel genuinely new. The pierogis stuffed with Gulf crab, the beet preparations, and the thoughtful wine list make this one of the most original restaurants in the city. One Michelin Star.

name: Tipitina's
description: The most storied music venue in New Orleans, open since 1977 in Uptown. Named after a Professor Longhair song, it's hosted every significant Louisiana musician and remains the essential room for funk, R&B, brass band, and roots music. Worth the Uber from the Quarter — shows here feel like the real city.

name: Maple Leaf Bar
description: Rebirth Brass Band has played every Tuesday night here for decades. That alone makes it mandatory. The rest of the week brings zydeco, funk, and roots music in a narrow, sweaty room on Oak Street. No tourists, no pretense — this is where New Orleans goes to dance.

name: Saturn Bar
description: Bywater dive bar open since 1960, unchanged in every detail — taxidermy, Christmas lights, hand-painted murals, and a jukebox that earns its keep. The bartenders have seen everything. Come after midnight and stay until it makes sense to leave. A New Orleans institution that never made the tourist map.
address: 3067 St Claude Ave, New Orleans, LA 70117

name: Lafitte's Blacksmith Shop Bar
description: One of the oldest surviving buildings in the Mississippi Valley, operating as a bar since 1772. No electricity — lit entirely by candles. Two blocks off Bourbon Street and invisible to most tourists who walk past it nightly. Order a Purple Drank, sit in the dark, and stop talking.

name: Snake & Jake's Christmas Club Lounge
description: No windows, no clocks, Christmas lights on year-round. Uptown dive bar open until 6am that operates entirely outside of normal time. The drinks are cheap, the regulars are loyal, and nothing that happens here gets repeated. Go when everywhere else has closed.

name: The Columns Hotel
description: Grand Victorian mansion on St. Charles Avenue with a wide front porch, rocking chairs, and the streetcar running past. Order a cocktail, sit outside, and watch the neighborhood drift by. One of the great New Orleans afternoon drinks — calm, beautiful, and entirely off the tourist circuit.
address: 3811 St Charles Ave, New Orleans, LA 70115

name: Twelve Mile Limit
description: Mid-City neighborhood cocktail bar that punches far above its weight — serious drinks at dive bar prices in a room full of locals. Named after the Prohibition-era boundary beyond which ships could legally sell alcohol. No tourists, no fuss, excellent Sazeracs.
address: 500 S Telemachus St, New Orleans, LA 70119

name: Pat O'Brien's
description: Invented the Hurricane in 1942 and has been serving them in the iconic 29-oz glass ever since. Touristy? Absolutely. Mandatory? Yes. The flaming fountain courtyard is genuinely fun, the piano bar dueling room is chaos in the best way, and you can't leave New Orleans without drinking one.
address: 718 St Peter St, New Orleans, LA 70116

name: Central Grocery
description: The original muffuletta — invented here in 1906 by Sicilian immigrant Salvatore Lupo. A round sesame loaf split and loaded with Italian meats, provolone, and olive salad. Order a half unless you're committed. The grocery shelves and deli case haven't changed in a century. Cash only.

name: Casamento's
description: Family-run oyster bar in Uptown since 1919, closed every summer when the oysters are off. The tile floors, the tin ceiling, the Casamento family behind the counter — nothing has changed. Order oysters on the half shell, fried oyster loaf, or the gumbo. Cash only. Seasonal hours — call first.

name: Drago's Seafood
description: Invented the charbroiled oyster — a style now copied across the Gulf Coast. Oysters on the half shell hit a screaming-hot grill, finished with garlic butter, herbs, and parmesan until the edges char and the butter pools in the shell. Order two dozen minimum. The original Metairie location is the one.

name: Port of Call
description: The best burger in New Orleans — a massive hand-formed patty on a toasted bun with a baked potato on the side, no exceptions. Open since 1963 on Esplanade, usually with a line. The Monsoon cocktail (rum, brandy, juice) is enormous and dangerous. Tourists walk past this constantly without stopping. Don't.

name: Liuzza's by the Track
description: Neighborhood bar and restaurant near the Fair Grounds racetrack and Jazz Fest site. The BBQ shrimp po-boy served in a frozen mug of Abita is a local legend. Beloved by Fair Grounds regulars, Uptown families, and anyone who knows to go one block off the tourist path. Cash only.
address: 1518 N Lopez St, New Orleans, LA 70119

name: Parasol's
description: Irish Channel neighborhood bar since 1952 with a legendary roast beef po-boy operation in the back. The bar is dark and welcoming, the po-boy is legitimately exceptional, and the neighborhood is real New Orleans. St. Patrick's Day here is a full city event. Tourists rarely venture this far down Magazine.

name: Angelo Brocato
description: Italian ice cream parlor in Mid-City open since 1905, run by the same family for four generations. Cannoli filled to order, spumoni by the slice, granita di limone, and gelato made from century-old recipes. One of the most beloved institutions in the city and almost completely unknown to visitors.
address: 214 N Carrollton Ave, New Orleans, LA 70119

name: Hansen's Sno-Bliz
description: The canonical New Orleans snowball — not a snow cone. Ernest Hansen invented the machine that shaves ice to a cloud-fine consistency in 1934; his granddaughter still runs the shop. The syrups are house-made, the flavors are real, and the experience is specific to this city. Seasonal (roughly April–September). Lines move fast.

name: Second Line Parades
description: Every Sunday from late September through late June, Social Aid and Pleasure Clubs parade through Black New Orleans neighborhoods with brass bands, matching suits, and decorated parasols and fans. The second line — the public who follows behind — is open to anyone. This is the living soul of the city and most tourists never know it exists. Check the schedule at nolasecondlines.com.

name: Bywater Neighborhood
description: The most interesting neighborhood in New Orleans right now — creatively dense, architecturally beautiful, and still largely tourist-free. Shotgun houses painted wild colors, local art studios, Bacchanal Wine, the Saturn Bar, and a farmers market on Saturdays. Walk it on a weekend morning before anyone else wakes up.

name: Oak Street
description: Uptown's neighborhood commercial strip — a six-block stretch anchoring Tipitina's, the Maple Leaf Bar, excellent po-boy shops, and independent boutiques. The Oak Street Po-Boy Festival every fall is one of the best free food events in the city. This is where Uptown locals actually spend their weekends.
